food_subgroup: Pasta dish with chicken and cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken carbonara pasta. FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for chicken carbonara in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for creamy chicken carbonara pasta dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of creamy chicken carbonara is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of pasta, chicken pieces, and cream sauce as typically plated at a casual dining or food delivery restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Carbonara Pasta — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; adapted with cream-sauce adjustment from USDA Foundation Foods entries for cooked pasta, cooked chicken breast, heavy cream, egg, and Parmesan cheese
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for creamy chicken carbonara as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted ingredient composite: cooked spaghetti (~45% by weight), cooked chicken breast (~25%), heavy cream (~15%), egg (~5%), Parmesan cheese (~5%), bacon (~5%). Energy and macros are calculated per 100 g of the combined dish. Secondary cross-check performed against USDA FDC entry 'Pasta with meat sauce' (FDC ID 168930) and 'Chicken pasta dish, NFS' (FDC ID 784410) to validate plausibility. Values are consistent with published ranges for cream-based pasta dishes.

gi_source.title: Spaghetti carbonara, white pasta with cream and egg sauce — GI analogous estimate based on 'Spaghetti, white, boiled 10–15 min' (GI 44, glucose reference) and cream-sauce attenuation effect documented in Atkinson et al. 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy chicken carbonara. GI estimated at 45 based on: (1) white spaghetti boiled 10–15 min has GI ~44 (University of Sydney GI Database, glucose=100 scale); (2) addition of fat (cream, cheese) and protein (chicken, egg) is well-documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a GI in the low-to-mid 40s for the composite dish; (3) analogous cream pasta dishes in the Sydney GI database cluster in the GI 40–50 range. GL per serving = (45 × 17.5 × 3.50) / 100 = 27.6 g, using 350 g serving size and 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g restaurant serving of creamy chicken carbonara provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly 49% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding extra salt or salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~27.6 g per serving) driven by refined pasta carbohydrates, partially attenuated by fat and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic index of 45 (low) reflects fat and protein attenuation of the pasta base, but the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 27.6 g, which is moderate-to-high; individuals with type 2 diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g), pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.9 g per 100 g; ~17.2 g per serving) from heavy cream, cheese, and bacon
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 350 g serving of creamy chicken carbonara contributes approximately 17 g of saturated fat, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose smaller portions, and consider requesting lighter cream or substituting with a tomato-based sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy Chicken Carbonara is a Western-style pasta dish served widely at casual dining restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore, including at establishments such as #Foodcoholic. It is an adaptation of the classic Italian carbonara, which traditionally uses eggs, Pecorino Romano or Parmesan cheese, guanciale (cured pork cheek), and black pepper. The Singapore restaurant version typically incorporates heavy cream to produce a richer, more indulgent sauce, and substitutes chicken breast or thigh pieces for the traditional pork, making it more accessible to a broader audience including those who do not consume pork. The dish is prepared by boiling spaghetti or fettuccine to al dente, sautéing chicken pieces with garlic and bacon or pancetta, and combining with a sauce made from heavy cream, beaten eggs, and grated Parmesan cheese, finished with cracked black pepper. The result is a rich, creamy, savoury pasta with a silky sauce that coats each strand of pasta. The flavour profile is umami-forward with notes of cheese, cream, and black pepper, and a mild sweetness from the cream. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 693 kcal, 40 g protein, 34 g fat (of which ~17 g is saturated), and 61 g carbohydrate. The dish is calorie-dense and moderately high in saturated fat and sodium, making it a food to consume in moderation for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), reflecting the attenuating effect of fat and protein on the pasta's glycemic response, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 27.6 g. The dish contains gluten, eggs, and dairy and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with these allergens. It is a popular comfort food and a common menu item across Western-style cafes and restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken carbonara?
Is chicken carbonara high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of carbonara pasta?
Is chicken carbonara suitable for hypertension?
What allergens are in chicken carbonara?
How much protein is in chicken carbonara?

confidence_reason: No exact match for creamy chicken carbonara exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted ingredient composite using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (cooked pasta, cooked chicken breast, heavy cream, egg, Parmesan, bacon), cross-validated against USDA FDC entries for analogous pasta dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on white spaghetti GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and published fat-protein attenuation evidence. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Indian', which appears to be a field survey platform categorisation error; the dish is Italian-inspired Western cuisine and has been corrected accordingly. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted ingredient composite (cooked spaghetti ~45%, cooked chicken breast ~25%, heavy cream ~15%, egg ~5%, Parmesan ~5%, bacon ~5%) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 45) based on white spaghetti GI ~44 from the University of Sydney GI Database, with fat-protein attenuation adjustment per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the ingredient sources used for this composite estimate. 5. Halal status is listed as false because the dish as described at the source restaurant may contain bacon or pancetta (pork-derived); halal certification would need to be confirmed per restaurant. 6. The dish is served at #Foodcoholic, 40 Circular Road, Singapore, via field survey at SGD 16.00. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the ingredient composite and should be treated as approximate. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate.
